title: Daily Health Briefing
intro: Good morning! Here's today's research digest across your tracked topics. Each section summarizes recent, reputable findings from sources like PubMed, JAMA, NIH, and the AASM — citations are linked as chips below each section.

sections.body:
The longevity field keeps shifting from animal models toward **human clinical trials**, and the most-watched candidate remains **rapamycin (an mTOR inhibitor)**.

- The **PEARL trial** (48-week, double-blind, placebo-controlled, NCT04488601) reported that intermittent **low-dose rapamycin is relatively safe** in healthy, normally-aging adults. Benefits were modest and sex-specific: women on 10 mg/week saw significant gains in **lean tissue mass** and reductions in self-reported **pain**; the 5 mg group reported better emotional well-being and general health. Visceral fat (the primary endpoint) did *not* change.
- **Urolithin A** continues to be studied for mitochondrial and metabolic health — a new trial (NCT06274749) launched mid-2026 to test its effect on insulin secretion, mitochondrial respiration, and the gut microbiome in adults 55+.
- **Senolytics** (e.g., fisetin) and **NAD+ precursors** (NR) remain promising but largely early-stage; most positive senolytic data are still from cells and animals.

Practical framing: prefer interventions backed by human RCTs, treat single-supplement "longevity" claims skeptically, and remember that benefits seen so far are generally **modest**.

sources.title: Influence of rapamycin on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results
Evaluation of urolithin A on mitochondrial respiration, insulin secretion and the gut microbiome in older adults
Top 10 Longevity & Anti-Aging Breakthroughs (senolytics, NAD+, rapalogs)

sections.body:
New 2026 evidence reinforces HIIT as a **time-efficient** way to boost fitness in women — with an important caveat about what it does and doesn't do.

- A **2026 systematic review & meta-analysis** (19 RCTs in middle-aged and older women) found HIIT **significantly improves VO2max** vs. controls (SMD = 1.20, 95% CI 0.86–1.54) with *high certainty* of evidence, consistent across age, body-mass status, and program length. Walking ability improved borderline-significantly.
- That same review found **no significant gain in muscle strength** from HIIT alone — so pair it with **resistance training** if strength/lean mass is a goal.
- In **postmenopausal women with overweight/obesity**, a 12-week trial (3 sessions/week) showed both HIIT and concurrent training reduced body weight, BMI, abdominal fat, triglycerides, total/LDL cholesterol, glucose, and insulin (HOMA-IR). **Concurrent (HIIT + resistance) training** produced slightly greater body-composition improvements.

Bottom line: intensity drives the cardio gains; add resistance work and ramp volume gradually with adequate recovery.

sources.title: Effects of HIIT on cardiopulmonary fitness and physical function in middle-aged and elderly women: systematic review & meta-analysis
HIIT and concurrent training on body composition and metabolic profile in postmenopausal women with overweight or obesity: a clinical trial
Effect of interval training on cardiometabolic health markers in postmenopausal women: systematic review with meta-analysis

sections.body:
**Pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT)** keeps its place as the evidence-based, **first-line** treatment for stress urinary incontinence (SUI) — and 2026 trials strengthen the case.

- A **2026 multicenter JAMA Network Open RCT** (764 first-time mothers, China) tested a structured prenatal program ("PEFLOW"). It **cut SUI incidence at 6 weeks postpartum** (8.7% vs. 13.9% in controls) and more than doubled the share of women with strong pelvic-floor muscle scores.
- A **2026 RCT** in women awaiting sling surgery found 6 weeks of **intensive preoperative PFMT** significantly improved muscle strength and incontinence severity — and **43% postponed their planned surgery** afterward.
- A **2026 network meta-analysis** (49 RCTs, 5,432 patients) ranked **PFMT + vaginal weights** highest for reducing objective leakage, with **biofeedback ± electrical stimulation** adding benefit for muscle strength.

Key point: *correct technique and consistency matter more than reps.* Confirm activation with a clinician or biofeedback, and supervised programs tend to outperform unsupervised ones.

sources.title: Pelvic Floor Workout for Preventing Stress Urinary Incontinence in Primiparous Women: A Randomized Clinical Trial
Effectiveness of Intensive Preoperative Pelvic Floor Muscle Training in Women with SUI Awaiting Surgery: A Randomized Controlled Trial
Effects of various exercise interventions for postpartum stress urinary incontinence: systematic review & network meta-analysis

sections.body:
The most relevant recent work for **HER2-positive, TP53/p53-mutated** breast cancer is a mechanistic study (PubMed, 2025) explaining why these "co-mutated" tumors are hard to treat — and how to exploit that.

- In mouse models, **HER2 + TP53 co-mutant** tumors grew faster and were **resistant to neratinib** alone, but showed increased histone acetylation, more open chromatin, and hyper-transcription of DNA-repair genes — making them **sensitive to the topoisomerase-I (TOP1) inhibitor payload** carried by **trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd / Enhertu)**.
- Combining **neratinib + T-DXd** was highly effective preclinically, supporting a rational strategy for these tumors.
- This combination is now in a **phase 1 trial** (NCI ETCTN 10495, NCT05372614). Puma Biotechnology's **AACR 2025** update reported the recommended phase 2 dose (T-DXd 5.4 mg/kg + neratinib escalated to 240 mg) and that a pharmacodynamic expansion cohort opened in 2025.

Important framing: this is **early-stage, investigational** evidence. "Alternative" should mean *evidence-based options discussed with an oncologist* — not a replacement for standard care. p53 itself remains largely "undruggable," with no approved p53-targeted therapy yet.

sources.title: HER2; p53 Co-mutated Cancers Show Increased Histone Acetylation and are Sensitive to Neratinib plus Trastuzumab Deruxtecan
HER2; p53 Co-mutated Cancers Sensitive to Neratinib plus T-DXd (full text)
Phase 1 trial of T-DXd in combination with neratinib in solid tumors with HER2 alterations (NCI 10495)

sections.body:
The newest guidance still favors **behavioral treatment over pills and supplements** for chronic, restless, or disjointed sleep.

- A brand-new **AASM clinical practice guideline** (published April 2026 in the *Journal of Clinical Sleep Medicine*) on *combination* therapy concluded: combining CBT-I with medication is better than **medication alone**, but combination is **not** better than **CBT-I alone** — which often produces meaningful, durable improvement without drug risks. **CBT-I remains first-line.**
- A 2025 best-evidence synthesis (28 high-quality sources) again confirmed **multicomponent CBT-I** (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation) is effective across individual, group, and **digital** formats.
- On supplements: the AASM continues to **suggest against melatonin** for chronic insomnia (insufficient efficacy evidence); melatonin is most useful for **circadian problems** like jet lag or shift work. Evidence for **magnesium** is limited.

Best-practice habits for restless/disjointed sleep:

1. Keep a **fixed wake time**, even on weekends.
2. Use the bed **only for sleep**; keep the room cool, dark, and quiet.
3. Dim lights and cut screens during a wind-down window.
4. If problems persist beyond a few weeks, see a provider to rule out sleep apnea and consider **CBT-I**.

sources.title: Combination treatment for chronic insomnia disorder in adults: an AASM clinical practice guideline
New guideline provides recommendations on combining treatments for chronic insomnia
Best evidence that CBT-I improves sleep quality in patients with chronic insomnia
